title: Bluetooth RC Car: HC-05 + L298N + DC Motors
subtitle: Cut the wires, take wireless control!
overview: In this exciting hands-on session, students build a smartphone-controlled Bluetooth Remote Control (RC) car. They will interface an HC-05 Bluetooth transceiver module with an Arduino Uno, route logic signals through an L298N H-bridge motor driver, and establish a wireless communication link to send real-time steering commands directly from a mobile application.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — breadboard layouts, serial terminal data logs, Bluetooth app setup screenshots, and assembled chassis.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Photo of HC-05 Bluetooth module showing TX/RX/VCC/GND pins clearly labeled
gallery.caption: HC-05 Bluetooth Module — The wireless ears of our robot chassis.

gallery.caption: Serial Communication — Passing numerical message tokens one byte at a time!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Clean overhead photo of wiring connections connecting Arduino to L298N driver and power supply
gallery.caption: Power Distribution Network — Separating delicate logic lanes from high-drain motor currents.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Smartphone screen photo showing the directional button layout used to pilot the car
gallery.caption: Mobile Interface Application — Mapping touchscreen button events to character data tags.

keyTopics: Wireless Bluetooth Spectrum — How data hops through radio frequency waves to link smart accessories across short spaces
Serial Communication (UART) — Cross-connecting communication coordinates: Arduino RX (Receive) links to HC-05 TX (Transmit) | TX to RX crosslink!
Voltage Divider Safety — Sneaking a resistor step-down circuit into the HC-05 RX line to lower Arduino's 5V signals to a safe 3.3V logic ceiling
Command Parsing Logic — Teaching the microchip brain to look for individual data codes (e.g., 'F'=Forward, 'B'=Back, 'L'=Left, 'R'=Right, 'S'=Stop)
H-Bridge Signal Mapping — Programming the L298N pins (IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4) to toggle direction patterns in response to serial input characters
Common Grounding Protocol — Maintaining a shared reference point across the phone's adapter node, logic boards, and battery packs
Debug Monitoring — Monitoring raw feedback lines via the serial monitor window to ensure hardware channels stay synchronized
Signal Range and Interference — Understanding how physical obstacles and metal shields degrade low-energy wireless fields

activity.title: Wireless Link Setup and Remote Chassis Pilot Run
parts: Assembled 2WD/4WD Robot Chassis with DC Motors
1kΩ and 2kΩ Resistors (for logic level translation setup)
Rechargeable Li-ion Power Cells (7.4V or higher)
Android Smartphone with Bluetooth Serial Controller App installed
steps: Signal Line Interfacing: Mount and hook up the HC-05 Module. Ensure the HC-05 TX goes directly to Arduino Pin 0 (RX), and use a resistor voltage divider to connect Arduino Pin 1 (TX) down to the HC-05 RX pin.
Motor Pipeline Connection: Bridge the L298N control block (IN1 through IN4) to designated Arduino digital pins, then secure motor outputs to the side terminal block lanes.
Code Provisioning Phase: Disconnect the HC-05 VCC line temporarily (to free up shared programming serial bands), upload the driver code structure via USB link, then reconnect wireless power.
Application Provisioning: Boot up the controller application, search for nearby devices, discover the 'HC-05' signature tag, and punch in the classic default password '1234' or '0000'.
Button Configuration: Map the application touch layouts so pressing arrow keys sends distinct characters ('F', 'B', 'L', 'R', 'S') down the radio channel.
The Launch Trial: Place the vehicle safely on a test lane, disconnect tether cables, open the control dashboard, and test wireless steering reflexes!

quiz.q: Why must you cross-connect communication pins between the Arduino and the HC-05 module (TX to RX, RX to TX)?
options: To increase the speed of the motor tracking wheels
Because one device's Transmit (TX) line must broadcast directly into the other device's Receive (RX) ears
To merge battery charging paths with logic data loops
To turn off the indicator lights when information is flowing
quiz.explanation: Crossed Signal Lanes! Communication requires a talker and a listener. The transmitter pin (TX) of the HC-05 shouts information out into space, which must be received by the listening ear (RX) pin of the Arduino module.

quiz.q: Why is it important to temporarily disconnect or disable the HC-05 module while uploading code to an Arduino Uno over USB?
options: The Bluetooth module might run out of battery juice
The computer's USB port will crash due to electrical overload
Both the USB link and the Bluetooth module share the exact same Serial Pins (0 and 1), creating data collision conflicts during uploads
The code compiler doesn't accept wireless connections
quiz.explanation: Shared Serial Line Conflict! The Arduino Uno relies on Pins 0 and 1 to flash programs from the computer over the USB cable. Leaving the HC-05 connected to those same tracks creates data collisions that stall the upload routine.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of placing a voltage divider circuit on the HC-05 module's RX pin?
options: To double the driving force of the rear motor wheels
To step down the Arduino's 5V signal to a safe 3.3V logic level, protecting the delicate internal circuits of the HC-05 module
To filter out bad background noise coming from room lights
To track black electrical lines on paper tracks
quiz.explanation: Logic Protection! While the Arduino Uno reads and writes 5V electrical signals, the internal processor inside the HC-05 operates at a lower 3.3V threshold. Sending an unregulated 5V down its RX line can bake its internal microchip layers over time.

quiz.q: If your phone sends the character code 'L' to the car, how does differential steering make the car turn LEFT?
options: It angles the front castor wheel using a steering wheel motor
It cuts power to or reverses the Left side motor while driving the Right side motor forward
It speeds up both motors simultaneously
It triggers an alert buzzer to sound from the dashboard
quiz.explanation: Differential Pivot! Since two-wheeled and four-wheeled chassis models don't feature adjustable mechanical rack steering, they execute turns by creating speed imbalances—slowing down the left track forces the active right track to pivot around it to the left.

quiz.q: What default passcode combination is usually configured out-of-the-box for pairing with an HC-05 module?
options: ABCD or XYZW
No password is required to connect
quiz.explanation: Factory Pairing Token! Almost all standard HC-05 and HC-06 Bluetooth communication chips are pre-flashed from factory benches with basic security authentication codes—either '1234' or '0000'.

quiz.q: What happens inside the Arduino software script when a button on the smartphone application is held down?
options: The microcontroller downloads a brand new program file
The phone beams continuous character codes via serial communication that are unpacked using the 'Serial.read()' command
The battery voltage automatically jumps to maximum levels
The Bluetooth antenna swaps channels to pick up radio stations
quiz.explanation: Serial Processing Stream! Pressing down an option button prompts the phone app to stream data strings over wireless waves. The Arduino reads these codes character-by-character to run matching conditional statements.

quiz.q: If the Bluetooth car drives forward fine but spins in reverse when given a 'Turn Right' command, what is the most likely issue?
options: The smartphone battery is running out of charge
The positive and negative motor wires are swapped on one of the motor driver terminals, reversing its rotation direction
The room's ambient lighting is interfering with the radio wave frequencies
The code needs to be recompiled into an ancient dialect
quiz.explanation: Motor Polarization Error! If direction logic breaks down on isolated wheel channels, it indicates a hardware polarity issue. Reversing the physical wire positions inside the L298N terminal blocks instantly corrects the code alignment.
